channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: atari
item.description:
Atari in weiqi is the state in which a stone or connected group has only one liberty remaining and is therefore under immediate threat of capture [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Atari matters because it turns a broad positional game into a forced tactical problem. When a group falls to one liberty, the next move often becomes urgent for both players: one side tries to capture, while the other tries to extend, connect, or create shape.

item.title: capture
item.description:
A capture in weiqi occurs when a move removes the last liberty from an opposing stone or connected group, after which those stones are removed from the board [@britannica2026go; @nagofed2026thisisgo].

Capture is the game’s basic tactical enforcement rule. Without it, enclosure would carry no force. But weiqi is not a pure capture race, because players usually capture in order to secure shape, reduce influence, or stabilize territory, not only to remove material.

item.title: connection
item.description:
A connection in weiqi is a relation between stones that makes them function as one group, sharing liberties and supporting each other’s defense [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Connection is structurally important because isolated stones are weak. A move that does not gain immediate territory may still be strong if it joins two unsettled groups, prevents a cutting attack, or turns a fragile shape into one that can live.

item.title: eye
item.description:
An eye in weiqi is an empty point or enclosed space inside a group’s shape that functions as an internal liberty and helps determine whether the group can live [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Eyes matter because they change the status of a group. A surrounded group with no secure eye shape may still be dead, while a well-connected group with two separate eyes is usually safe. This is why players often defend shape even when no immediate capture is on the board.

item.title: handicap
item.description:
A handicap in weiqi is a rule adjustment, usually extra Black stones placed before play begins, that lets players of unequal strength compete in a meaningful game [@britgo2026whatisgo; @britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Handicap is one reason weiqi supports play across large skill differences without becoming a different game. The stronger player does not receive more pieces or special powers during play. Instead, the weaker player begins from a materially stronger board position.

item.title: ko
item.description:
Ko in weiqi is a repetition constraint that forbids a player from making the immediate recapture that would recreate the previous board position [@britgo2025default].

Ko matters because it prevents endless local loops while preserving the tactical possibility of capture. Once a ko appears, the fight usually stops being purely local: the blocked player must move elsewhere first, which can turn the rest of the board into leverage for the eventual return to the ko point.

item.title: komi
item.description:
Komi in weiqi is the point compensation added to White’s final score to offset Black’s first-move advantage [@britgo2025default; @britgo2026whatisgo].

Komi matters because weiqi is asymmetrical at the start: Black moves first. Without compensation, that initiative would distort results across many even games. Komi is therefore a scoring adjustment, not a tactical rule inside local fights.

item.title: liberty
item.description:
A liberty in weiqi is an empty intersection directly adjacent to a stone or connected group of stones [@nagofed2026thisisgo]. Liberties determine whether a group remains on the board. When the last liberty is removed, the group is subject to capture.

Liberties matter because they convert board geometry into tactical pressure. A move may look quiet in territorial terms while still being urgent if it reduces a group from two liberties to one. This is why local fights in weiqi often turn on counting available adjacent points rather than on the nominal size of a group.

item.title: life and death
item.description:
Life and death in weiqi is the analysis of whether a group can secure enough stable shape, usually through eyes, to avoid capture, or whether the opponent can force it off the board [@britgo2026howtoplaygo].

Life-and-death reading is one of the game’s central skills because local shape decides global outcomes. A large territorial framework can collapse if one key group dies. A small group can become strategically decisive if it lives inside the opponent’s area.

item.title: territory
item.description:
Territory in weiqi is the set of vacant points effectively enclosed by one player’s stones and unavailable to the opponent except at unacceptable cost [@britannica2026go; @nagofed2026thisisgo].

Territory is the strategic heart of the game because it turns placement into scoring. A move may increase territory directly by closing space, or indirectly by making an invasion weak. This is why players often choose moves that neither attack nor defend a single group immediately but instead reshape the balance of large empty regions.
